title: Module Check: Space Complexity and Recursion Depth

questions.question: What determines the call stack depth of a recursive DFS on a tree, and what are the best- and worst-case values?
questions.answer: The height h of the tree. Best case: O(log n) for a balanced tree. Worst case: O(n) for a degenerate (linear chain) tree.

questions.question: Why is `sys.setrecursionlimit(10**6)` insufficient for very deep Python recursion?
questions.answer: Python's limit controls the internal counter but the OS limits the C call stack (typically ~8MB, ~20K–40K Python frames). A very deep recursion hits the OS stack limit before Python's, causing a segfault rather than a clean RecursionError.

questions.question: What is the correct fix for a Python RecursionError on a large DFS?
questions.answer: Convert to iterative DFS using an explicit stack list (heap-allocated, not limited by the OS call stack). Each frame's local state (node, children index) goes on the explicit stack instead of the call stack.

questions.question: For a balanced binary tree with n nodes, compare BFS and DFS space complexity.
questions.explanation: BFS holds the entire widest level in its queue — O(n/2) = O(n) for a balanced tree. DFS only holds one path from root to leaf on the call stack — O(log n) for balanced. DFS is more space-efficient on balanced trees.

questions.question: When is BFS preferred over DFS despite its higher space cost?

options.A: When you need to detect cycles
options.B: When you need the shortest path in an unweighted graph
options.C: When the graph has very high branching factor
options.D: When you need to perform topological sort

questions.explanation: BFS explores by level, so the first path found to any node is guaranteed the shortest (fewest edges). DFS may find a longer path first and never revisit. Cycle detection and topological sort both typically use DFS.
